**Suggestion: Improve Weekly Boss and Heroic Hostile Nest Rewards**

I think players should have a fairer and more consistent way to earn valuable rewards from weekly bosses and Heroic Hostile Nest runs.

For example, weekly bosses could have a chance to reward a purple OS Watch after every 10 runs, while Heroic Hostile Nest could have a chance to reward a high-end gear mod or attachment after every 10 runs as well.

Right now, it feels like there isn’t enough consistency in how players are rewarded for the time they put in. Adding a small guaranteed or semi-guaranteed reward system would make grinding feel more worthwhile without making the rewards too easy to obtain.

Hello, I would like to submit feedback regarding the current UI experience in The Division Resurgence, especially on PC. The screen feels extremely cluttered with too many icons, text elements, and UI details taking up space during gameplay. The weapon icons and the PC-related information on the bottom-left side of the screen are especially too large and distracting. They obstruct the view of the game and make it harder to enjoy the actual environment, combat, and overall visual experience.
I strongly suggest adding more UI customization options so players can remove, resize, reposition, or adjust the opacity of individual HUD elements. There should be a way to turn off unnecessary text, reduce visual clutter, and customize the screen layout based on personal preference. Right now, the UI feels overwhelming, almost like being inside a helicopter control board with too many things displayed at once. I want to experience the game in a beautiful, clean, and comfortable way, but the current lack of customization makes the screen feel messy and distracting.

content: Hope u read my 30 min brainstormed feedback <:Timeout:1369815742064627802> <:Rogue_icon:998521840991477821>

* Another concern is content cadence. We are living in an AI-driven world where development speed is accelerating, and AAA mobile games are releasing free content constantly.
* The Division franchise has been around for nearly 8 years, yet many of the same community concerns continue to be raised. Players often feel that feedback is acknowledged but not meaningfully implemented.
* Active, long-term players understand the game deeply. Ignoring what engaged endgame players are asking for creates frustration and gradually reduces trust in the direction of the game.
* New content is what keeps players engaged. Loot is important, but content is the fuel that keeps the community active.
* If new AI-assisted development tools, procedural content systems, and modern game engines can accelerate content creation, then players expect a faster delivery cadence than in the past.
* Repeating the same seasonal model, the same Hostile Nest activities, and the same grind loops with minor variations is not enough to maintain long-term engagement.
* The future is new maps, new missions, new enemy behaviors, new game modes, dynamic world events, and unexpected experiences that make players want to log in every day.
* Players can accept bugs, balance issues, and occasional setbacks if they are consistently receiving fresh content and meaningful reasons to return.
* The constant cycle of “testing,” “development,” and “future plans” only works for so long. Eventually players want to see results delivered into the live game.
* Content pace matters. The gaming market is more competitive than ever, and players have endless alternatives. If content delivery remains slow while competitors release new experiences every few weeks, players will naturally move on.
* The biggest threat to a live-service game is not difficulty, balance, or monetization—it is player boredom.
* Fresh content creates excitement. Excitement creates engagement. Engagement keeps communities alive.

* Personally, if you want to keep daily players active and improve the looter experience, I would focus on better drops rather than forcing players to level up every piece of gear and every weapon after Level 40.
* Make Green Gear Sets, Exotics, and God-Roll items the primary chase. Players enjoy grinding when they find something powerful that is immediately usable.
* Most players want to try new weapons and gear as soon as they drop. Having to level them up first creates friction and reduces excitement.
* Looking at build diversity and analytics, it feels like only around 10% of weapons and 10% of gear sets are being used regularly. Many items are simply not viable compared to the meta.
* There are very few versatile builds available today. Increasing build diversity would encourage experimentation and keep players engaged longer.
* The fun should come from finding better rolls and stronger gear, not from repeatedly leveling existing gear and weapons.
* The majority of the current player base consists of Division 2 veterans. Many of them have already invested thousands of hours into progression systems.
* Have you seen other successful looter games intentionally slow down player progression in this way? Most successful games focus on rewarding players with meaningful loot upgrades rather than additional gear-leveling systems.
* For mobile specifically, inventory management and organization should be optimized for two-thumb gameplay. Consider studying inventory systems from other popular mobile games and adopting the best patterns rather than reinventing them.
* Keep the gameplay loop simple: Play → Get Exciting Loot → Improve Build → Try New Builds → Repeat.
* If progression becomes too focused on leveling gear instead of finding great loot, you may gradually lose players over time.

After taking a deeper look at the weapon module system, I believe the current storage limit of 100 weapon modules is far too restrictive and should be increased.

As players start optimizing their builds, many different attribute combinations become valuable. A module that is useless for one build may be best-in-slot for another. For example, Weapon-focused builds will often look for combinations such as All Weapon DMG + Weapon Blast/Pierce/Shredding DMG, while Skill-focused builds will look for the Skill equivalents. This quickly creates a large number of valuable module combinations worth keeping.

As a result, players are often forced to destroy potentially valuable modules simply because they run out of space. Storing modules on weapons is only a partial workaround and quickly becomes difficult to manage.

I would strongly suggest increasing the weapon module capacity, as the current limit discourages build experimentation, collection, and long-term progression across multiple specializations and playstyles.

(A better Filter / Sort System would also be great 🙂 )

Feedback and Suggestions for The Division Resurgence Endgame
As the player base grows and more people reach the endgame, I believe there is an important issue regarding build optimization and item progression.
Currently, a weapon is defined by five key elements:
Talents (2)
Attributes (2)
Type / Main Stat Requirement (Firearms / Electronics / Stamina)
For endgame players who are trying to fully optimize and min-max their builds, if even one or two of these five elements are bad, the weapon becomes nearly useless. This makes the loot hunt extremely frustrating because finding a truly optimal item is incredibly rare.
Some ideas that could improve the endgame experience:
Expand and improve the crafting system, allowing players to have more control over item optimization.
Create more reasons to log in regularly, especially in the Dark Zone, to increase player engagement and activity.
Further develop the vendors/markets, with meaningful weekly refreshes and better item rotations.
Continue expanding and improving the Dark Zone experience.
Introduce a new difficulty level, as Legendary (19,200 GS requirement) has become too easy for many veteran players who have progressed very quickly.
Continue investing in events, seasonal activities, and endgame content to keep players engaged between updates.
I am happy to see marketing efforts on platforms like TikTok, and I believe the player base will continue to grow from season to season. However, I think the points above are important for maintaining long-term player retention and providing a rewarding endgame experience for dedicated players

One quality of life improvement I would like to suggest is adding a dedicated keybind for rolling or dodging.

Currently, rolling shares input behavior with other actions, and this can become a major issue for Bulwark players. During intense fights when tanking multiple enemies, attempting to reposition or dodge can sometimes cause the shield to be lowered unintentionally, leaving the player exposed at the worst possible moment.

Having a standalone roll or dodge keybind would provide more control, improve responsiveness, and prevent accidental shield drops during high pressure encounters. This would especially benefit Bulwark players while still being a useful accessibility and control option for all players.

I believe this would make combat feel smoother and more reliable without affecting game balance.

If I could change one thing about the end game in The Division Resurgence, it would definitely be the reward and loot system. Right now, the problem is not the difficulty of the content, but the feeling that players can spend days grinding, farming legendary missions, doing Dark Zone extractions, or even spending real money, and still end up with terrible rolls, duplicate rewards, or no meaningful progression. The game would feel much more rewarding and fair if there were better RNG protection systems, guaranteed progression after heavy grinding, reroll options for premium rewards, badluck protection for rare drops, and more targeted farming for specific builds. The gameplay itself is genuinely fun, but the inconsistency of rewards is what makes the endgame frustrating for many players.
